name: Iodine Pit Recovery
description: Teaches apprentices about xenon poisoning after a power reduction. The reactor is brought to partial power, then power is reduced sharply. Students must observe the xenon build-up and attempt recovery. Demonstrates why the RBMK design was particularly dangerous at low power.

action.message: EXERCISE: Bring reactor to approximately 70% power by adjusting rod withdrawal. Allow 30 seconds to stabilise.
EXERCISE: Now reduce power to below 30% by inserting rods. Observe the xenon and iodine gauges.
ALERT: Xenon-135 rising above 0.25 — reactivity suppression increasing. Power recovery becoming difficult.

ALARM: Xenon pit developing. To recover, you must withdraw almost all rods — this leaves very low ORM (Operational Reactivity Margin). This is exactly the situation at Chernobyl Unit 4 at 01:23 on 26 April 1986.
DISCUSSION POINT: Why is it dangerous to have nearly all rods withdrawn? What happens if a void fraction spike occurs now?
